---
title: "Overlay, Displays, and Spoken Commands"
---

## Why AppKit owns the window

SwiftUI is used to describe content, but AppKit's `NSPanel` supplies the exact
window behavior:

- borderless;
- non-activating;
- floating above ordinary windows;
- visible when the dashboard loses focus;
- allowed in all Spaces and full-screen auxiliary contexts;
- independently placed on a selected display.

`NSHostingView<CaptionOverlayView>` embeds the SwiftUI hierarchy inside that
panel.

## Interaction layer

A transparent `OverlayInteractionView` sits above the hosted content. Its
`hitTest` returns itself only for:

- a 42-point strip along the top;
- a 48-by-48-point lower-right resize area.

All other points return `nil`, so the overlay does not intercept the entire
screen area. `acceptsFirstMouse` allows dragging without activating the app.

Mouse movement is measured in global screen coordinates. The controller stores
the panel frame at drag start and applies total translation from that fixed
frame. This avoids integrating many small event deltas, which previously made
the panel lag behind the cursor.

## Geometry

Each display's frame is persisted in `UserDefaults` under a key containing its
Core Graphics display ID.

Restoration always clamps:

```text
width  = clamp(saved width,  minimum width,  visible width)
height = clamp(saved height, minimum height, visible height)
x      = clamp(saved x, left margin,  right edge - width)
y      = clamp(saved y, bottom margin, top edge - height)
```

The visible frame excludes the menu bar and Dock. This prevents an unplugged or
reconfigured monitor from stranding the overlay outside reachable space.

## Rendering and scrolling

The overlay receives only:

- three latest visible finalized captions;
- one provisional caption;
- optional current question;
- pending question count;
- font size.

Final captions are white. Provisional text is dimmer and italic. A
`ScrollViewReader` assigns an invisible bottom anchor and scrolls to it when a
content-version string changes. This prevents long captions from ending in an
unreachable ellipsis.

Questions occupy a separate orange card with their own bounded scroll view.
Waiting questions use a compact orange count badge without exposing text.

## Display privacy

The selected `NSScreen` determines where the panel appears. In extended-desktop
mode, the professor can place it on a private display while the projector shows
other material. In mirrored mode, macOS duplicates the display, so the
application cannot make mirrored pixels differ.

## Spoken command pipeline

Commands are recognized from Voxtral text before it enters the timeline or
Gemma:

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    V[Voxtral cumulative provisional] --> R[Recognizer]
    R -->|command actions| UI[Overlay/question state]
    R -->|remaining lecture text| T[Timeline]
    T --> G[Gemma]
    T --> A[Archive]
```

Defaults are:

- `Bonjour soleil bleu`: show;
- `Bonjour soleil rouge`: hide;
- `Bonjour soleil blanc`: clear;
- `Bonjour question suivante`: open oldest pending;
- `Bonjour question terminée`: dismiss current.

The recognizer lowercases, removes accents, normalizes separators, and permits a
small edit distance for sufficiently long words. The multiword prefix reduces
accidental matches in normal computer-science speech.

Voxtral provisional output is cumulative. A command visible in three successive
provisional strings must execute once, not three times. The application stores
the number of already-processed commands and applies only the new suffix.

Potential partial commands are temporarily hidden so “Bonjour soleil…” does not
flash as a caption before the color arrives.

## Clear is a view operation

Clear does not delete the timeline. It records the next sequence number that may
be shown and creates a transcription boundary. Existing segments remain
available for:

- Gemma correction;
- session export;
- diagnostics;
- later audit.

If the overlay was visible, it resumes when a new caption arrives.
